Category: --- DAILY LOG FRAMEWORK: the structure that organizes all daily field data collection ---

Description: The parent record for all daily field data — one per project per day. Acts as the container that links all log sections (manpower, equipment, weather, delays, notes, etc.) to a single date and project. Completion and distribution tracking ensure the log was finalized and sent to stakeholders. The daily log is the legal record of what happened on site each day.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contractually required on most projects; AIA A201 §3.11 references site documentation obligations
Common Values: One header per project per day; completed/distributed status; completable flag controls whether log is ready for finalization; day_of_week for calendar analysis
Source Systems: Procore, Raken, FieldWire, Rhumbix, paper forms
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_completion table is the header/parent record. Fields: log_date, completed (boolean), completed_at, completed_by_id, distributed (boolean), distributed_at, completable, missing_day, variance, day_of_week, admin_actual_start_date, admin_projected_finish_date. All child log tables reference daily_log_completion_id as FK.
Benchmarking Use: Daily log completion rate (% of project days with completed logs); distribution rate; average days between log date and completion; missing day frequency
Notes: The foundation of field operations data. A project with low log completion rates has unreliable field data across ALL sections. completion rate is the single best metric for field data quality. missing_day flag identifies gaps. Cross-domain: every field insight depends on log completion consistency.

Description: The template configuration that defines which log sections are required, optional, or hidden for a project. Determines what data gets collected — a project with 'Delays' set to optional may have sporadic delay records, while one with it required will have consistent data. Section configuration is the key to data consistency across projects and companies.
Industry Standard: No standard; company/project-specific configuration
Common Values: Required, Optional, Hidden per section; configurable by project or company-wide template; sections: Manpower, Equipment, Deliveries, Delays, Notes, Safety Violations, Weather, Visitors, Waste/Dumpsters, Inspections, Accidents, Phone Calls, Scheduled Work, Plan Revisions, Productivity, Quantities
Source Systems: Procore (template configuration), company-level settings
Procore Coverage: Medium — configuration is a system setting, not a data table. daily_log_construction_reports table tracks workforce reporting configuration. Section-level completion can be inferred from child table population rates.
Benchmarking Use: Section completion rate by project; companies with standardized vs. ad-hoc templates; section adoption rate across portfolio
Notes: Template standardization is the single biggest lever for field data quality. Companies that mandate all sections get 3-5x more data per project. Cross-company benchmarking requires template normalization — can't compare delay frequency if one company doesn't track delays.

Category: --- FIELD NOTES & COMMUNICATIONS: narrative records, verbal communications, and document receipts captured in the daily log ---

Description: Free-text narrative entry by field personnel documenting site conditions, work progress, issues, and events — the superintendent's daily diary. Multiple notes may be entered per day by different team members. Notes can flag issue days when something notable occurred (safety concern, weather event, schedule impact, owner directive). The richest unstructured data source in field operations — frequently used for keyword-based signal extraction across rework, material shortage, and design change topics.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; AIA A201 §3.11 references site documentation obligations
Common Values: Free text narrative; typical length 20-200 words; issue day flag (boolean); entries by superintendent, project engineer, safety manager, or APM
Source Systems: Procore, Raken, FieldWire, paper daily reports
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_note table with comment (STRING) and is_issue_day (BOOLEAN). ~130K+ records. Widely adopted. Primary NLP signal source.
Benchmarking Use: Notes per day per project; issue day frequency; keyword density for specific topics (safety, weather, schedule, material)
Notes: The primary free-text signal source for NLP-based analysis. Keyword extraction hits ~0.09% for specific topics like material shortages. is_issue_day flag provides structured overlay on unstructured text. Cross-domain: feeds insight discovery across rework, material shortages, and design changes.

Description: Record of field-related phone calls — caller, recipient, subject, and notes. Documents verbal communications for the project record. Less formal than written correspondence but can capture critical decisions, commitments, or directives made verbally. Phone calls between the superintendent and subcontractors, inspectors, or the design team are common daily occurrences that rarely get documented elsewhere.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; good documentation practice
Common Values: One entry per call; caller, recipient, subject, notes (call summary), time
Source Systems: Procore, paper logs, phone systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_phone_call table with caller, recipient, subject, notes. Adoption varies — not all projects track phone calls in the daily log.
Benchmarking Use: Phone call volume per project; calls by party (owner, architect, sub) over time
Notes: Low individual analytics value but call patterns reveal communication dynamics. Frequent architect calls may indicate design issues. Cross-domain: supplements correspondence records in Documents & Communications.

Description: Field record of updated drawing revisions received on site — which drawings were updated, to what revision, and when the field team received them. Critical for confirming that construction crews are working from current documents. Discrepancies between issued revisions and field-held revisions are a major source of construction errors and rework.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (document distribution); CSI (document management)
Common Values: One entry per revision received; drawing number, revision identifier (Rev C, ASI #4), date received
Source Systems: Procore, Bluebeam, PlanGrid, Newforma, paper transmittals
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_plan_revision table with drawing_number, revision, date_received. Links to drawing revisions in project management. Adoption varies.
Benchmarking Use: Field revision receipt lag (days between drawing issue and field receipt); revisions received per week by phase
Notes: Tracks WHEN the field received updated drawings — not the drawing content itself. Long receipt lags mean the field may be building from outdated drawings. Cross-domain: connects to drawing revisions in Documents & Communications.

Category: --- WORKFORCE & LABOR: who is on site, which trades, how many hours ---

Description: Daily record of workforce on site by trade and vendor — captures trade name, number of workers, hours per worker, total man-hours, and the vendor/subcontractor responsible. One entry per trade per vendor per day. The most direct source of trade-level workforce data in field operations. Superintendent or field engineer enters daily as part of the log.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contractually required on most projects
Common Values: One entry per trade/vendor/day; trade_name (free text), num_workers (integer), hours (decimal per worker), man_hours (decimal total), vendor_name/vendor_id, cost_code_id, notes
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_manpower), Raken, FieldWire, Rhumbix, paper forms, timecards (separate system)
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_manpower table is well-structured and widely adopted. Fields: trade_name (STRING, primary trade signal), num_workers (INT), hours (DECIMAL per worker), man_hours (DECIMAL total), vendor_id (LONG FK), vendor_name (STRING), cost_code_id (LONG), cost_code_name, notes, location_id, contact_id, contact_name, manpower_logs_date (DATE).
Benchmarking Use: Workers per day by trade; man-hours per day/week/month; peak headcount; trade mix over time; workers-per-million-dollars; man-hours by cost code
Notes: The strongest trade-level signal in Procore. trade_name is free text — needs keyword normalization to canonical trades (e.g., 'Electricians' → 'Electrician'). Not all companies fill it consistently. When populated, it's the most direct path from field data to the Resources taxonomy (Trades). Cross-domain: feeds productivity calculations, safety rate denominators, and schedule resource loading.

Description: Certified payroll and workforce diversity reporting record — tracks hours by worker classification (apprentice, journeyman, foreman) and demographic category (minority, women, veteran, local) per trade and vendor. Required on public projects with prevailing wage and diversity requirements. Separate from the manpower log — focuses on compliance reporting rather than daily operations.
Industry Standard: Davis-Bacon Act (federal prevailing wage), state prevailing wage statutes, local hire and diversity requirements
Common Values: One entry per vendor/trade/day; classification hours: apprentice_hours, journeyman_hours, foreman_hours, other_hours; counts: number_of_apprentice_workers, number_of_journeyman_workers, number_of_foreman_workers, number_of_other_workers; diversity: minority_hours, women_hours, veteran_hours, local_city_hours, local_county_hours, first_year_hours
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_construction_reports), LCPtracker, Elation Systems, eMars, state-specific portals
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_construction_reports table has extensive structured fields for classification and diversity tracking. Fields: trade_name, trade_id, vendor_name, vendor_id, all classification/diversity hour and worker count columns. Specific to compliance-heavy projects.
Benchmarking Use: Total man-hours by classification; apprentice ratio; diversity participation rates; local hire percentage; compliance rate against project-specific targets
Notes: Critical for public projects. Prevailing wage violations carry heavy penalties. Diversity tracking (minority hours, women hours, veteran hours) increasingly required on private projects too. trade_id (LONG) exists but may not resolve to a lookup table. Cross-domain: feeds certified payroll reporting and workforce diversity metrics.

Description: Structured record of individual worker hours — employee, date, hours worked, cost code, project, overtime classification. More granular than manpower log (individual workers vs. crew-level). Timecards are the source of truth for payroll and labor cost accounting. Captured by field foremen or self-reported by workers.
Industry Standard: FLSA (Fair Labor Standards Act), state labor laws, union agreements for overtime and classification rules
Common Values: One entry per worker per day; hours (regular, overtime, double-time), cost_code, project, employee_id, job_title
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.timecard_entries — NOT READY), Rhumbix, Hcss, ExakTime, paper timesheets
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.timecard_entries table exists but is not yet available for import. When ready, it will be the most granular labor tracking source.
Benchmarking Use: Hours per worker per day; overtime ratio; labor cost per unit; cost code distribution; worker utilization rate
Notes: NOT READY for import. When available, timecards will be the authoritative source for labor cost and productivity at the individual worker level. Currently, daily_log_manpower is the best available approximation at the crew level.

Description: Master record for a field worker — name, role, job title, trade classification, certifications, company assignment. The identity record that timecards, crew memberships, and workforce planning reference. Includes safety training and certification status.
Industry Standard: No universal construction standard; OSHA 10/30 certification tracking
Common Values: Employee ID, name, job_title, company_id, trade classification, certification status
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.employees — NOT READY), HRIS systems, Rhumbix, ExakTime
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.employees table exists but is not yet available. When ready, it will provide individual-level trade classification.
Benchmarking Use: Workforce size by trade; certification compliance rate; employee turnover
Notes: NOT READY. When available, employee-level trade classification will be more reliable than inferring trade from manpower log free text. Currently, crew-level data from daily_log_manpower is the best available.

Description: Grouping of workers into a named crew assigned to a specific scope of work — crew name, members, lead/foreman, trade, and assignment. Crews are the operational unit of field labor management. A plumber crew of 6 is scheduled, tracked, and managed as a unit, not 6 individuals.
Industry Standard: No standard; company-specific crew management
Common Values: Crew name, crew lead/foreman, member list, trade, assigned scope/area
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.crew_memberships — NOT READY), Rhumbix, Excel
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.crew_memberships exists but is not available. Crew-level tracking currently inferred from manpower log (vendor + trade + num_workers).
Benchmarking Use: Crew size by trade; crew continuity (same crew on project over time); crew-to-scope assignment
Notes: NOT READY. Crew-level workforce planning is how superintendents actually think about labor — not individual workers. When available, links to workforce planning (assignments, requests).

Category: --- EQUIPMENT: what machines and tools are on site and how they're being used ---

Description: Daily record of equipment on site — equipment name, hours operating, hours idle, and inspection status. One entry per piece of equipment per day. Captures both utilization (operating vs. idle) and safety compliance (inspected flag, inspection_time). Equipment is identified by free-text name — no standardized equipment taxonomy in field data.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart N (Cranes), Subpart O (Motor Vehicles), Subpart P (Excavations — equipment requirements)
Common Values: One entry per equipment per day; equipment_name (free text — brand names common), hours_operating (decimal), hours_idle (decimal), inspected (boolean), inspection_time, vendor_name, cost_code_id, notes
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_equipment), Tenna, HCSS, equipment rental company portals, paper logs
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_equipment table is well-structured. Fields: equipment_name (STRING, primary identifier), hours_operating (DECIMAL), hours_idle (DECIMAL), inspected (BOOLEAN), inspection_time (STRING), equipment_logs_date (DATE), cost_code_id, cost_code_name, notes, vendor_name. equipment_id and equipment_identification_number exist but are untyped (sparse).
Benchmarking Use: Equipment utilization rate (operating / (operating + idle)); equipment count by type; equipment on site over time; uninspected equipment rate; equipment cost per hour
Notes: equipment_name is free text — needs keyword classification into canonical types (12 categories per Resources taxonomy: crane, excavator, loader, aerial lift, forklift, scaffolding, etc.). Brand names are common ('JLG', 'Genie', 'CAT 320'). Inspected flag is a daily safety compliance signal. Cross-domain: equipment utilization feeds cost analysis and schedule resource loading.

Description: Catalog record for a piece of equipment — type, model, serial number, capacity, ownership/rental status, maintenance schedule. The identity record for equipment that field logs, timecards, and maintenance records reference. Equipment master data lives in fleet management systems.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; company-specific equipment management
Common Values: Equipment ID, name, type, model, serial number, capacity, owner (company or rental), maintenance status
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.equipment — NOT READY), Tenna, HCSS, fleet management systems, rental company systems
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.equipment table exists but is not available. When ready, it will provide the authoritative equipment catalog with structured type/model data.
Benchmarking Use: Fleet size; equipment age; maintenance compliance; rental vs. owned ratio
Notes: NOT READY. Currently, equipment identification relies on free-text equipment_name in daily_log_equipment. Structured equipment master data would enable reliable classification without keyword matching.

Description: Structured time record for individual equipment — hours by category (operating, idle, maintenance, travel), cost allocation, and operator assignment. More granular than the daily equipment log (includes cost allocation and maintenance time). Source of truth for equipment cost accounting.
Industry Standard: No universal standard
Common Values: Equipment ID, date, hours by category, cost code, operator, project
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.equipment_timecard_entry — NOT READY), Tenna, HCSS, rental billing records
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.equipment_timecard_entry exists but is not available. When ready, provides granular equipment cost tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Equipment cost per hour; utilization by cost code; idle time analysis
Notes: NOT READY. Currently, daily_log_equipment provides operating and idle hours at the day level. Equipment timecards would add cost allocation and more precise utilization tracking.

Category: --- MATERIALS & DELIVERIES: what arrives on site and when ---

Description: Daily record of materials received on site — contents description, delivery source, tracking number, time, and comments. One entry per delivery event per day. The contents field is a free-text description of what was delivered. Deliveries are major daily events that affect staging, storage, and workflow.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; good practice per AIA A201 site management obligations
Common Values: One entry per delivery/day; contents (free text — 'Steel beams, W12x26', '40 CY ready-mix concrete', 'Drywall — 500 sheets 5/8'), delivery_from, tracking_number, time, comments, vendor_name
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_delivery), Raken, paper logs
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_delivery table is structured. Fields: contents (STRING — primary material signal), delivery_from (STRING), tracking_number (STRING), time (STRING), comments (STRING), delivery_logs_date (DATE), vendor_name. Widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Deliveries per day; delivery volume over project timeline; delivery-to-installation lag
Notes: contents field is free text describing what was delivered — the most direct material receipt signal in field operations. Cross-domain: feeds Materials taxonomy (what arrived), Schedule (was it on time?), and Financial (did delivery match PO?). Compare to submittals (what was approved) and workforce.materials_receipt (structured receipt — NOT READY).

Description: Structured tracking of material shipments from supplier/fabricator to jobsite — shipment date, expected arrival, contents, quantity, and carrier. Tracks the supply chain between purchase order and site delivery. Separate from delivery log (which records what actually arrived on site).
Industry Standard: No universal standard; PO terms govern delivery expectations
Common Values: Shipment ID, PO reference, contents, quantity, ship date, expected arrival, carrier, tracking number
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.materials_shipment — NOT READY), supplier portals, freight tracking systems
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.materials_shipment exists but is not available. When ready, it links POs to delivery tracking.
Benchmarking Use: Shipment-to-delivery time; late shipment rate; shipments per PO
Notes: NOT READY. Currently, delivery tracking relies on daily_log_delivery (what arrived) and submittals with required_on_site_date (what was expected). The gap between expected and actual delivery is critical for material shortage detection.

Description: Formal acknowledgment that materials arrived on site matching the purchase order — quantity verification, condition inspection, and storage location. More structured than the delivery log (which is a field observation). Receipt records are the procurement system's confirmation of delivery.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; PO terms govern acceptance
Common Values: Receipt ID, PO reference, item, quantity ordered, quantity received, condition, storage location, received_by
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.materials_receipt — NOT READY), ERP material modules, paper receiving tickets
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.materials_receipt exists but is not available. When ready, it closes the PO-to-delivery loop.
Benchmarking Use: Receipt-to-PO match rate; damaged/short shipment rate; time from receipt to installation
Notes: NOT READY. The delivery log is currently the only signal for material arrival. Structured receipt data would enable quantity verification and damage tracking.

Category: --- WEATHER: conditions that affect work and drive delays ---

Description: Daily record of observed weather conditions on the project site — temperature, precipitation, wind, sky conditions, humidity, ground conditions, and whether weather caused a delay. Multiple readings may be taken throughout the day. Both qualitative descriptions (from superintendent) and measured values (from weather stations or manual readings) are captured.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contract-specific weather provisions; NOAA data for verification
Common Values: One entry per day (or multiple time periods); temperature (descriptive + measured high/low/avg), precipitation (descriptive + measured since midnight), wind (descriptive + measured avg/gust/max), sky (clear, partly cloudy, overcast, etc.), ground (dry, wet, muddy, frozen, snow), humidity (avg/high/low), is_weather_delay (boolean), calamity (storm events), comments
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_observed_weather_condition), NOAA weather data, Davis weather stations, manual field observation
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_observed_weather_condition is one of the most structured daily log tables. Fields: sky (STRING), temperature (STRING descriptive), precipitation (STRING descriptive), wind (STRING descriptive), ground (STRING), calamity (STRING), is_weather_delay (BOOLEAN), comments (STRING). Measured values: measured_temperature_high/low/average (INT), measured_precipitation_since_midnight (DOUBLE), measured_wind_speed_average/gust/max (DOUBLE/INT), measured_humidity_low (INT), average_humidity (INT), maximum_humidity (INT), average_dewpoint (INT). weather_logs_date (DATE).
Benchmarking Use: Weather delay frequency; precipitation days per project; temperature extremes by month; weather delay rate by geography; is_weather_delay correlation with schedule slip
Notes: Richest weather dataset in construction software. Dual signal: qualitative superintendent observations + quantitative measured values. is_weather_delay is a structured boolean — the primary weather-delay signal. Cross-domain: weather drives daily_log_delay entries (delay_type = 'Weather'), affects productivity rates, and is the most common excusable delay claim basis. NOAA data can validate superintendent observations for dispute resolution.

Category: --- DELAYS: what stopped or slowed work and for how long ---

Description: Record of a delay event during construction — structured type, duration, start/end time, comments, and location. Delay entries document when work was stopped or significantly slowed and why. The delay_type field is a structured enum with both standard categories and company-defined custom values. Duration is in days (or fractional days). This is the primary delay tracking mechanism in field operations.
Industry Standard: AACE 29R-03 (Forensic Schedule Analysis), AIA A201 §15.1.6 (Claims for Additional Time), contract-specific delay provisions
Common Values: delay_type values (structured enum): Weather, Existing Conditions, Material, Owner Directive, Non-Conformance, Safety Event, Quality, Equipment + custom company-defined values. Duration: median 5.0 days for Material delays. Start/end time for partial-day delays.
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_delay), P6 (delay activities — less structured), paper delay logs
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_delay table is highly structured. Fields: delay_type (STRING — structured enum, primary categorization), duration (DOUBLE), start_time (STRING), end_time (STRING), comments (STRING — 98.8% populated on Material delays), daily_log_entries_date (DATE), location_id, vendor_name. 84K records for Material type alone, 4,351 cos.
Benchmarking Use: Delay days by type; delay frequency by project type/geography; material delay rate; weather delay days per month; delay cost (delay days × daily general conditions rate)
Notes: The strongest structured delay signal in Procore — delay_type is a structured enum requiring zero NLP. Material delays (84K records, 4,351 cos) validated as primary signal for material shortage detection. Cross-domain: LINKED_TO weather (delay_type = 'Weather' correlated with is_weather_delay), schedule (delay days affect critical path), financial (delay days × daily GC rate = delay cost), and claims (delay logs are primary evidence for time extension requests).

Category: --- PRODUCTIVITY & QUANTITIES: how much work was completed ---

Description: Daily record of quantities delivered and used/installed — tracks material flow from delivery to installation by vendor and commitment line item. Captures previously_delivered, previously_used, quantity_delivered (today), and quantity_used (today). Links to commitments for scope tracking. Measures the rate at which paid work is being installed.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; earned value concepts per PMI PMBOK
Common Values: One entry per vendor/item/day; quantity_delivered (today), quantity_used (today), previously_delivered, previously_used, commitment_id, commitment_line_item_id, vendor_id, notes
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_productivity), Rhumbix, HCSS, paper logs
Procore Coverage: Low — daily_log_productivity table exists and is structured but has very low adoption (622 cos as of validation). Fields: quantity_delivered (DECIMAL), quantity_used (DECIMAL), previously_delivered, previously_used, commitment_id (LONG), commitment_line_item_id (LONG), vendor_id (LONG), notes, productivity_logs_date (DATE).
Benchmarking Use: Installed quantity per day; cumulative installed vs. planned; productivity rate by trade; delivery-to-installation lag
Notes: Extremely low adoption (622 cos) limits benchmarking value. When populated, provides the strongest earned-value signal in field operations — directly links installed quantities to commitments. Cross-domain: feeds earned value analysis (% complete based on quantities) and resource productivity metrics. The low adoption makes daily_log_manpower (man-hours) a more reliable proxy for productivity.

Description: Daily record of installed quantities by cost code — quantity, unit of measure, description, and cost code. Distinct from productivity log (which tracks vendor-level delivery and installation). Quantity logs measure what was put in place by cost code — closer to a field-measured percent complete for budget line items.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; analogous to field-measured quantities for earned value
Common Values: One entry per cost code/day; quantity (DOUBLE), unit (STRING — LF, SF, CY, EA, etc.), description (STRING), cost_code_id, cost_code_name, location_id, vendor_name
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_quantity), field measurement logs, paper quantity sheets
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_quantity table is structured with quantity, unit, and cost_code linkage. Fields: quantity (DOUBLE), unit (STRING), description (STRING), cost_code_id (LONG), cost_code_name (STRING), location_id (LONG), quantity_logs_date (DATE). Adoption higher than productivity log but still not universal.
Benchmarking Use: Installed quantities per cost code per day; cumulative quantities vs. budget; unit rate (quantity / man-hours)
Notes: More adoption than daily_log_productivity but still sporadic. cost_code linkage is the key value — enables quantity-based percent complete by budget line item. Cross-domain: feeds Financial Instruments (budget line item % complete), Schedule (earned value from quantities), and Resources (unit productivity rates).

Category: --- SAFETY: field-level safety observations, violations, and incidents ---

Description: Daily log record of a safety violation observed during field walkthroughs — subject, comments, issued_to (person or company), compliance_due date, and safety_notice type. Lighter weight than a formal observation or incident — this is the superintendent's daily safety diary. Subject field is often generic ('None', 'N/A') or brief.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety Standards), company safety programs
Common Values: One entry per violation/day; subject (STRING — 66% null per validation), comments (STRING), issued_to (STRING — person name), vendor_name (STRING — no FK), compliance_due (DATE), safety_notice (STRING — notice type), time
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_safety_violation), iAuditor/SafetyCulture, paper violation notices
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_safety_violation table exists and is Ready. Fields: subject (STRING — 66% null), comments (STRING), issued_to (STRING), vendor_name (STRING — free text, no FK), compliance_due (DATE), safety_notice (STRING), safety_violation_logs_date (DATE). Structured but data quality is poor — subject mostly generic.
Benchmarking Use: Safety violation frequency per project; violations by vendor; compliance due date adherence; repeat violation rate
Notes: Validated as UNUSABLE for recurrence tracking (insight #11) — subject 66% null, mostly noise. vendor_name is free text with no FK, preventing reliable trade attribution. Better safety signals come from observation.hazard_name (structured OSHA taxonomy) and inspection_items (structured status). Keep for volume metric but don't rely on for detailed analysis.

Description: Daily log record of an accident or safety event — involved person, involved company, time, comments, and location. This is the field-level first report — the formal incident record (project_management.incident, incident_record) provides the detailed OSHA-reportable information. Accident log entries are the superintendent's immediate documentation.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1904 (Recording and Reporting), OSHA 300/300A/301 forms
Common Values: One entry per event/day; involved_name (STRING), involved_company (STRING), time (STRING), comments (STRING), accident_logs_date (DATE)
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_accident), incident management systems, paper first reports
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_accident table is structured but lightweight. Fields: involved_name (STRING), involved_company (STRING), comments (STRING), time (STRING), accident_logs_date (DATE). The full incident detail is in incident/incident_record tables.
Benchmarking Use: Accident frequency; accidents by company; time-of-day distribution
Notes: Lightweight first report — the formal incident system (incident, incident_record, incident_action) provides the detailed analysis data including injury type, body part, recordability, and corrective actions. Cross-domain: accident log entries should link to formal incidents, but no direct FK exists in the daily log table.

Category: --- INSPECTIONS: field-level daily inspections recorded in the log ---

Description: Inspection event recorded as part of the daily log — inspecting entity, inspection type, inspector name, start/end time, and comments. Captures regulatory and third-party inspections that happen during the day (fire marshal visit, concrete testing, structural steel inspection, utility inspection). Distinct from the formal inspections tool (project_management.inspections) which has structured checklists and line items.
Industry Standard: Building code inspection requirements, OSHA, project specifications
Common Values: One entry per inspection event/day; inspection_type (STRING — 'Structural', 'Concrete', 'Electrical', 'Fire Protection', 'MEP', etc.), inspecting_entity (STRING — 'City', 'Fire Marshal', 'Third Party', company name), inspector_name (STRING), start/end times, comments, area
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_inspection), inspector reports, regulatory portals
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_inspection table is structured. Fields: inspection_type (STRING), inspecting_entity (STRING), inspector_name (STRING), start (STRING), end (STRING), comments (STRING), area (STRING), location_id (LONG), inspection_logs_date (DATE). Adoption varies by project.
Benchmarking Use: Daily inspection count; inspections by type; inspection pass/fail (from comments, not structured); regulatory inspection frequency
Notes: Records the OCCURRENCE of an inspection, not the detailed results. The formal inspections tool (inspections + inspection_items) provides structured pass/fail with checklist items. Cross-domain: daily log inspection entries document when inspections happened; the inspections tool documents what was found. Both are needed for complete inspection tracking.

Category: --- VISITORS: who was on site that isn't part of the project team ---

Description: Daily record of non-project visitors on the construction site — visitor identity, purpose, arrival/departure times, and comments. Required for site security, safety compliance (visitors must be briefed on site hazards), and liability documentation. Visitors include owners, architects, inspectors, utility representatives, and unauthorized persons.
Industry Standard: OSHA general duty clause (site safety for visitors), company safety policies, contract terms for site access
Common Values: One entry per visitor/day; subject (STRING — visitor name or purpose), details (STRING — visit description), start/end (STRING — arrival/departure), vendor_name (STRING), visitor_logs_date (DATE)
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_visitor), site access control systems, paper sign-in sheets
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_visitor table is structured. Fields: subject (STRING), details (STRING), start (STRING), end (STRING), vendor_name (STRING — not a true vendor, often the visitor's organization), visitor_logs_date (DATE). Adoption varies.
Benchmarking Use: Visitor count per day; visitor types (owner visits, architect visits, inspector visits); visitor frequency over project lifecycle
Notes: Visitor patterns reveal project dynamics — frequent owner visits during finishes indicate engaged clients. Architect visit frequency correlates with design issue volume. Inspector visits cluster around milestones. vendor_name field is repurposed for visitor organization (not a true vendor FK). Low analytics value individually, but visitor patterns + other signals can indicate project phase transitions.

Category: --- WASTE & ENVIRONMENTAL: construction waste tracking and disposal ---

Description: Daily record of construction waste generated and removed — material type, quantity, disposal method, disposal location, and vendor. Tracks what waste was produced, how much, and where it went. Required for environmental compliance (LEED waste diversion, stormwater compliance) and cost tracking (dumpster rental, hauling costs).
Industry Standard: LEED MR Credit (Construction Waste Management), EPA RCRA (hazardous waste), local waste regulations
Common Values: One entry per waste event/day; material (STRING — 'Concrete rubble', 'Drywall scrap', 'Mixed C&D', 'Hazardous'), approximate_quantity (DOUBLE), method_of_disposal (STRING — 'Recycling', 'Landfill', 'Hazmat disposal'), disposal_location (STRING), description (STRING), vendor_name, time
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_waste), waste hauler records, LEED tracking spreadsheets
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_waste table is structured. Fields: material (STRING — waste material type), approximate_quantity (DOUBLE), method_of_disposal (STRING), disposal_location (STRING), description (STRING), vendor_id (LONG), vendor_name, waste_logs_date (DATE). Adoption varies by project and sustainability requirements.
Benchmarking Use: Waste volume per project; waste diversion rate (recycled / total); waste by material type; waste cost per SF
Notes: Increasingly important for sustainability reporting. LEED MR credit requires 50-75% waste diversion. material field describes the waste type — enables diversion analysis. Cross-domain: feeds sustainability metrics, environmental compliance documentation, and cost tracking (waste hauling costs as direct costs).

Description: Daily record of dumpster activity — dumpsters delivered and removed from the site. Tracks the containers, not the waste contents (that's the waste log). Dumpster management is a general conditions cost item. Multiple dumpsters may be on site simultaneously for different waste streams (mixed C&D, concrete, metal, cardboard).
Industry Standard: No standard; general conditions management
Common Values: One entry per dumpster event/day; quantity_delivered (INT — dumpsters in), quantity_removed (INT — dumpsters out), comments, vendor_name (hauling company), dumpster_logs_date (DATE)
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_dumpster), waste hauler records
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_dumpster table is structured. Fields: quantity_delivered (INT), quantity_removed (INT), comments (STRING), vendor_name (STRING), vendor_id (LONG), dumpster_logs_date (DATE). Simple but functional.
Benchmarking Use: Dumpsters per week; dumpster turnover rate; dumpster cost per month
Notes: Operational metric. High dumpster turnover during demolition and interior rough-in. Dumpster rental and hauling is a significant general conditions line item — typically $500-1,500 per pull. Cross-domain: feeds general conditions cost analysis.

Category: --- SCHEDULED WORK: what was planned to happen today vs. what actually happened ---

Description: Daily record of subcontractors scheduled to work — whether they showed up, how many workers, hours, and what tasks they were assigned to. The 'showed' boolean is the simplest plan-vs-actual signal in field operations. Scheduled work entries link to schedule tasks (via daily_log_scheduled_work_task) connecting the daily log to the CPM schedule.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; good practice per lean construction / Last Planner System
Common Values: One entry per vendor/day; resource_name (STRING — sub name), showed (BOOLEAN — did they show up?), workers (INT), hours (DOUBLE), task_names (STRING — comma-separated task descriptions), hourly_rate (DOUBLE), reimbursable (BOOLEAN), comments, vendor_name
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_scheduled_work), Last Planner boards, Excel lookaheads
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_scheduled_work table is structured with the critical 'showed' boolean. Fields: resource_name (STRING), showed (BOOLEAN), workers (INT), hours (DOUBLE), task_names (STRING), hourly_rate (DOUBLE), reimbursable (BOOLEAN), comments (STRING), work_logs_date (DATE), vendor_id, vendor_name, daily_log_completion_id.
Benchmarking Use: Plan percent complete (showed = true / total scheduled); no-show rate by vendor; actual vs. scheduled workers; scheduled work utilization rate
Notes: The showed boolean is the Last Planner System's 'PPC' (Percent Plan Complete) equivalent — did the scheduled work happen? No-show rate by vendor is a reliability signal. task_names provides the planned activity description. Cross-domain: LINKED_TO schedule tasks via daily_log_scheduled_work_task, which provides schedule_task_id FK for direct CPM linkage.

Description: Junction record linking a scheduled work entry to one or more CPM schedule tasks — the bridge between 'what was planned today' (daily log) and 'what the schedule says' (CPM). Each link captures the task name, current percentage, and schedule_task_id FK to the formal schedule. Enables daily plan-vs-actual at the activity level.
Industry Standard: No standard; unique Procore construct
Common Values: One entry per task per scheduled work entry; task_name (STRING), task_percentage (INT), schedule_task_id (LONG FK to schedule_task), task_row_number
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_scheduled_work_task)
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_scheduled_work_task table exists with schedule_task_id (LONG) FK to the schedule. Fields: task_name, task_percentage (INT), schedule_task_id, daily_log_scheduled_work_id (FK to parent).
Benchmarking Use: Task-level plan-vs-actual; daily percent complete updates; schedule activities with field confirmation
Notes: The critical bridge between field operations and schedule. schedule_task_id links directly to schedule_task (legacy) or scheduling_activity (new). No equivalent in P6 — this is Procore-native field-to-schedule linkage. Cross-domain: the primary LINKED_TO path from Field Operations to Schedule taxonomy.

Category: --- FIELD PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT: structured ways to measure work output ---

Description: Structured measurement of field production — actual quantities produced vs. planned, by trade and activity. Distinct from daily_log_productivity (which tracks vendor-level delivery/installation) and daily_log_quantity (which tracks cost-code-level quantities). Field productivity records are designed for crew-level output tracking.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; lean construction metrics, unit rate analysis
Common Values: Crew, activity, planned quantity, actual quantity, unit, variance, notes
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.field_productivity — NOT READY), Rhumbix, HCSS, paper
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.field_productivity exists but is not available for import. When ready, it will provide crew-level production data for unit rate analysis.
Benchmarking Use: Unit production rate (quantity / man-hours); planned vs. actual; crew efficiency factor
Notes: NOT READY. Currently, productivity must be inferred from daily_log_manpower (man-hours) + daily_log_quantity (installed quantities) + daily_log_productivity (vendor quantities). Structured field productivity would close this gap.

Description: Recorded measurement of work actually completed — the 'actuals' side of earned value. Tracks what was installed in the field, by scope item, providing the physical percent complete that drives payment and forecasting.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (Earned Value Management), AACE
Common Values: Scope item, quantity completed, unit, measurement date, measured by
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.actual_production_quantities — NOT READY)
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.actual_production_quantities exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: Actual vs. budgeted quantities; production rate trends; quantity-based percent complete
Notes: NOT READY. When available, this is the authoritative 'what was actually built' signal. Currently, daily_log_quantity is the closest approximation.

Description: Planned quantity for a scope item — the 'budget' side of production tracking. Defines how much work is expected to be installed, creating the baseline for earned-value comparison.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK, AACE
Common Values: Scope item, budgeted quantity, unit, source (estimate, schedule, budget)
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.budgeted_production_quantities — NOT READY)
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.budgeted_production_quantities exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: Budget-to-actual variance by scope item; estimate accuracy
Notes: NOT READY. When available, budget vs. actual production quantities enable true earned value at the field measurement level.

Category: --- TIME & MATERIAL TRACKING: T&M tickets for extra work and force account ---

Description: Time and material ticket documenting extra work performed outside the base contract — captures labor hours, equipment hours, and materials used for change work or disputed scope. T&M tickets are the contractor's documentation of actual costs for work that will be billed on a cost-plus basis. Each ticket is a daily record requiring owner or GC sign-off.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 §7.1.3 (for cost-plus changes), contract-specific T&M provisions
Common Values: One ticket per day per extra work item; status (Draft, Submitted, Approved, Disputed), total cost, approval chain
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.time_and_material_entries — NOT READY), paper T&M tickets, Excel tracking
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.time_and_material_entries exists but is not available. When ready, provides structured T&M tracking.
Benchmarking Use: T&M tickets per project; T&M cost as % of contract; approval cycle time; dispute rate
Notes: NOT READY. T&M tracking is a major pain point — disputed T&M tickets are a top source of claims. Currently tracked via paper, email, and Excel on most projects. Cross-domain: T&M costs flow into change events and change orders.

Description: Labor portion of a T&M ticket — worker classification, hours, rate, and total labor cost. Multiple labor entries per ticket (different workers, different rates). Classification (foreman, journeyman, apprentice) determines the billing rate per contract schedule of rates.
Industry Standard: Contract schedule of rates, prevailing wage (if applicable)
Common Values: Worker, classification, hours (regular, OT, DT), rate, total, date
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.time_and_material_labors — NOT READY)
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.time_and_material_labors exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: T&M labor hours by classification; T&M labor cost per ticket; average hourly rate
Notes: NOT READY. Labor is typically the largest component of T&M costs.

Description: Equipment portion of a T&M ticket — equipment type, hours, rate, and total equipment cost. Equipment rates follow the contract schedule or industry published rates (Blue Book, FEMA).
Industry Standard: Blue Book rental rates, FEMA equipment rates, contract-specific rates
Common Values: Equipment type, hours, rate, total, date
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.time_and_material_equipments — NOT READY)
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.time_and_material_equipments exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: T&M equipment hours; equipment cost per ticket; rate compliance
Notes: NOT READY. Equipment rates are often disputed — Blue Book vs. actual rental cost.

Description: Material portion of a T&M ticket — material description, quantity, unit cost, and total material cost. Materials must be documented with receipts or invoices. Markup on materials is governed by contract terms (typically 10-15% overhead and profit).
Industry Standard: Contract markup provisions
Common Values: Material, quantity, unit cost, total, receipt/invoice reference
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.time_and_material_materials — NOT READY)
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.time_and_material_materials exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: T&M material cost per ticket; material markup compliance
Notes: NOT READY. Material costs on T&M tickets require receipt documentation for audit.

Category: --- WORKFORCE PLANNING: scheduling and allocating labor across projects ---

Description: Allocation of a worker or crew to a specific project for a date range — the workforce planning layer above daily manpower logs. Assignments represent the planned labor allocation; manpower logs record the actual. The gap between assigned and actual is a key management signal.
Industry Standard: No standard; company-specific workforce management
Common Values: Worker/crew, project, start date, end date, role, trade
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.assignments — NOT READY), workforce planning tools, Excel
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.assignments exists but is not available. When ready, it connects workforce planning to field operations.
Benchmarking Use: Planned vs. actual workforce; assignment fill rate; worker utilization across projects
Notes: NOT READY. When available, planned vs. actual workforce comparison enables capacity planning and identifies understaffing/overstaffing. Cross-domain: connects to daily_log_manpower (actual) for variance analysis.

Description: Request for additional workers or specific trades — submitted by field teams to the home office or labor pool. Tracks the demand signal for labor that may not yet be assigned. Request status (open, filled, unfilled) indicates workforce availability.
Industry Standard: No standard
Common Values: Trade requested, quantity, project, date needed, urgency, status (open, filled, unfilled)
Source Systems: Procore (workforce.requests — NOT READY), workforce planning tools, email/phone
Procore Coverage: None (Not Ready) — workforce.requests exists but is not available.
Benchmarking Use: Open requests by trade; time to fill; unfilled request rate
Notes: NOT READY. Workforce requests are the leading indicator of labor shortages. Unfilled requests = constraint on schedule execution.

Category: --- FIELD REPORTING & DISTRIBUTION: how daily data is compiled and shared ---

Description: The act of sending the completed daily log to stakeholders — tracks who received the compiled report, when it was distributed, and by whom. Distribution is a formal project management action — it makes the daily log an official project record. Some contracts require daily distribution to the owner and architect.
Industry Standard: Contract-specific distribution requirements; AIA A201 §3.11
Common Values: Distributed to: owner, architect, GC home office, project team; distribution method: email, Procore notification, printed copy
Source Systems: Procore (daily_log_completion — distributed flag and distributed_at timestamp)
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_completion tracks distribution as a first-class field: distributed (BOOLEAN), distributed_at (TIMESTAMP), distributed_by_name (STRING).
Benchmarking Use: Distribution rate (% of completed logs that were distributed); time from completion to distribution; projects with consistent distribution
Notes: Distribution rate is a project management discipline metric. Logs completed but not distributed have less contractual weight. Cross-domain: distribution triggers notification workflows to the project team.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from field operations data ---

Description: Percentage of project days with completed daily logs — completed logs / total project days × 100. The single best metric for field data quality across all sections. Projects below 80% completion rate have unreliable field data. Measured from daily log header records with completion status.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; contractually required completion on most projects
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 95-100%; typical: 70-90%; below 70% = unreliable data
Source Systems: Procore, Raken, FieldWire
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_completion table tracks completed (BOOLEAN) and log_date. missing_day flag identifies gaps.
Benchmarking Use: Compare log completion rates across project types and sizes to identify data quality thresholds for reliable benchmarking
Notes: The data quality gatekeeper for all field operations analysis. Every insight that depends on field data should filter by log completion rate. Projects with low rates produce sampling bias, not true signal.

Description: Percentage of completed daily logs distributed to stakeholders — distributed / completed × 100. Distribution transforms the daily log from internal notes into an official project record with contractual weight. Undistributed logs have weaker standing in disputes and claims.
Industry Standard: Contract-specific distribution requirements; AIA A201 §3.11
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 100% of completed logs; typical: 80-95%
Source Systems: Procore, email systems, project portals
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_completion tracks distributed (BOOLEAN), distributed_at (TIMESTAMP), distributed_by_name.
Benchmarking Use: Compare distribution rates across projects and companies to identify documentation discipline
Notes: Completion without distribution = incomplete documentation. Cross-domain: distribution triggers stakeholder notification and creates the audit trail for claims.

Description: Maximum number of workers on site in a single day during the project — MAX(SUM(workers per trade per vendor) per day). Indicates project peak complexity and coordination burden. Peak headcount drives safety staffing requirements, site logistics planning, and temporary facility sizing.
Industry Standard: OSHA staffing ratios; company safety staffing policies
Common Values: Typical peaks: 50-100 (small), 100-300 (mid), 300-800 (large), 800+ (mega). Usually occurs during MEP rough-in or interior finishes.
Source Systems: Procore, Raken, FieldWire, site access systems
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_manpower provides num_workers per trade/vendor/day. Aggregation yields daily totals.
Benchmarking Use: Compare peak headcount relative to project size and type to benchmark coordination complexity and safety staffing needs
Notes: Peak headcount defines the project's maximum coordination complexity. Cross-domain: feeds safety staffing ratios (Safety Manager per X workers) and site logistics planning.

Description: Average number of workers on site per active construction day — AVG(daily headcount) across construction days, excluding non-work days. Indicates sustained labor intensity throughout the project. More stable than peak headcount for long-term comparison.
Industry Standard: No universal standard
Common Values: Typical averages: 20-50 (small), 50-150 (mid), 150-400 (large). Varies significantly by phase.
Source Systems: Procore, Raken, FieldWire, timekeeping systems
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_manpower provides daily worker counts. Aggregated over active project days.
Benchmarking Use: Compare average headcount per million dollars across project types to benchmark labor intensity
Notes: More meaningful than peak for cost analysis — average × duration × loaded rate ≈ total labor cost. Cross-domain: feeds man-hours-per-million-dollars productivity metrics.

Description: Percentage of equipment time spent operating vs. idle — operating hours / (operating + idle hours) × 100. Calculated per equipment type and per project. Low utilization indicates over-provisioning or scheduling inefficiency. High idle time drives unnecessary rental cost.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; equipment management best practices
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; typical: 40-70% for most equipment; tower cranes: 30-50%; earthwork equipment: 60-80%
Source Systems: Procore, Tenna, HCSS, fleet management systems
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_equipment provides hours_operating and hours_idle per equipment per day.
Benchmarking Use: Compare equipment utilization rates by type and project phase to identify over- or under-provisioned equipment
Notes: equipment_name is free text — requires keyword classification into canonical types before meaningful benchmarking. Cross-domain: feeds equipment cost analysis (idle hours × daily rate = waste).

Description: Percentage of scheduled work that was actually performed — showed / total scheduled × 100. The Last Planner System's primary reliability metric. Measures whether the weekly or daily work plan was executed as planned. No-show rate = 1 - PPC. A leading indicator of schedule reliability.
Industry Standard: Lean Construction Institute; Last Planner System; Ballard 2000
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; target: 85%+; typical: 60-75%; top performers: 80-90%
Source Systems: Procore, Last Planner boards, Touchplan, Excel
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_scheduled_work provides showed (BOOLEAN) per vendor/day. Direct PPC calculation.
Benchmarking Use: Compare PPC across projects and vendors to identify reliable vs. unreliable trade partners and predict schedule performance
Notes: PPC below 60% predicts schedule slippage. Vendor-level PPC identifies chronically unreliable trade partners. Cross-domain: strongest operational leading indicator for schedule performance.

Description: Number of recorded delay events per active project month, segmented by delay type — total delays / active months. Enables comparison of delay exposure across projects, geographies, and seasons. Material, weather, and owner-directed are the three most common categories.
Industry Standard: AACE 29R-03 (Forensic Schedule Analysis)
Common Values: Typical: 2-5 delay events per month (mid-size project). Varies heavily by geography (weather) and project type.
Source Systems: Procore, P6 (delay activities), paper delay logs
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_delay with delay_type (structured enum) and duration. 84K+ Material records alone.
Benchmarking Use: Compare delay frequency by type across geographies and seasons to set realistic schedule contingencies
Notes: delay_type is a structured enum — zero NLP needed. Cross-domain: delay frequency × daily general conditions rate = delay cost estimate. Feeds schedule risk analysis and claims documentation.

Description: Percentage of project days with weather-related delays — weather delay days / total project days × 100. Geographic and seasonal comparison metric. Combines weather observation data (conditions) with delay records (impact). Distinct from bad weather days — only counts days where weather actually stopped or significantly slowed work.
Industry Standard: Contract-specific weather provisions; NOAA climate data for regional baselines
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; typical: 5-15% of project days; varies by region (Pacific NW: 15-25%, Southwest: 3-8%, Northeast winter: 20-30%)
Source Systems: Procore, NOAA weather data, Davis weather stations
Procore Coverage: High — daily_log_observed_weather_condition (is_weather_delay BOOLEAN) and daily_log_delay (delay_type = Weather). Dual signal.
Benchmarking Use: Compare weather delay rates across regions and seasons to calibrate schedule duration and weather contingency
Notes: Two signals: weather log is_weather_delay flag and delay log weather entries. Cross-domain: feeds schedule contingency calculation and is primary evidence for excusable delay claims.

Description: Percentage of construction waste recycled or diverted from landfill — recycled/diverted volume / total waste volume × 100. Required for LEED MR credit (50-75% target). Increasingly required by local ordinances and owner sustainability mandates. Measured from waste log entries by disposal method.
Industry Standard: LEED MR Credit (Construction Waste Management); EPA RCRA; local waste diversion ordinances
Common Values: Expressed as percentage; LEED target: 50-75%; typical: 40-60%; top performers: 75-95%
Source Systems: Procore, waste hauler records, LEED tracking spreadsheets
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily_log_waste provides material type and method_of_disposal. Method categorization enables diversion calculation.
Benchmarking Use: Compare waste diversion rates across project types and sizes to benchmark sustainability performance
Notes: Requires method_of_disposal to distinguish recycling from landfill. Cross-domain: feeds sustainability reporting and environmental compliance documentation.
